clinical_indications: Vaginal bulge of thin hymenal tissue with a dark or bluish hue caused by hematocolpos at puberty
Distended tubes with fimbrial ends closed by adhesions (hematosalpinx) in late/neglected cases
Pelvic or abdominal pain (cyclic or acute)
Abdominal mass, urinary retention, dysuria, constipation, and dyschezia
Hydrocolpos or mucocolpos in neonatal period, presenting as a bulging, translucent, or yellow mass at the introitus
Periodic lower abdominal pain (may be continuous), primary amenorrhea and urinary symptoms (e.g., frequency, dysuria, retention of urine)
Physical examination revealing a bulging hymen

clinical_thresholds.note: Surgical intervention is necessary only in symptomatic prepubertal patients
If physical examination reveals a bulging hymen AND ultrasonography reveals hematocolpos

preauth.label: Detailed Clinical notes with history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings and advice for admission

ppd_preauth.q: Are detailed clinical notes, including history, symptoms, signs, physical examination (including local examination), indication for procedure, advice for admission, and planned line of treatment, available?
Did the patient present with periodic lower abdominal pain (which may be continuous), primary amenorrhea, or urinary symptoms (such as frequency, dysuria, or retention of urine)?
Was the USG pelvis confirming the diagnosis of imperforate hymen with hematocolpos?
Was clinical evaluation and imaging indicative of surgery documented?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and treatment details available?
Are the detailed procedure/operative notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge available?
Were the USG pelvis findings indicative of surgery?

common_queries: Clinical evaluation and/or imaging not documented as indicative of surgery.
Patient not documented as symptomatic (e.g., lacking periodic lower abdominal pain, primary amenorrhea, or urinary symptoms).
USG pelvis report missing or not confirming the diagnosis of imperforate hymen with hematocolpos.
Detailed clinical notes or planned line of treatment missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Detailed procedure/operative notes missing or incomplete at claim submission.

additional_information.special_empanelment: Facilities with well-equipped operation theatre, anesthesia and anesthetist availability
clinical_key_pointers: Imperforate hymen is due to failure of disintegration of the central cells of the Müllerian eminence that projects into the urogenital sinus.
surgical_options: Surgical management of clinically significant hymenal variations involves excision of the hymenal tissue and rarely is associated with long-term sequelae.
Surgical intervention is necessary only in symptomatic prepubertal patients.
